romania.title: CM 2026. SUA, Mexic şi Canada anunţă măsuri de sănătate publică privind călătoriile, în contextul epidemiei de Ebola
romania.summary: Statele Unite, Mexic şi Canada au anunţat joi măsuri coordonate de sănătate publică privind călătoriile pentru persoanele care sosesc din regiunile africane expuse celui mai mare risc de infectare cu Ebola. Autorităţile din cele trei ţări au emis o declaraţie comună, în scopul de a proteja cetăţenii şi vizitatorii pe durata Cupei Mondiale de fotbal, relatează Reuters.

romania.title: Portugalia înregistrează un record de temperatură pentru o zi de mai, de peste 40 de grade Celsius
romania.summary: Portugalia a înregistrat un nou record de căldură pentru o zi din luna mai, cu o temperatură maximală de 40,3 grade Celsius măsurată miercuri în Mora, un oraş din centrul ţării, a anunţat joi agenţia meteorologică portugheză, precizând că precedentul record, care era de 40 de grade Celsius, fusese stabilit în mai 2001, informează agențiile …

romania.title: Ucrainean rătăcit în Munţii Maramureşului, recuperat: „Era excelent echipat şi foarte bine pregătit fizic, a luptat pe front”
romania.summary: Ucraineanul rătăcit într-o zonă de prăpăstii şi cascade din Munţii Maramureşului, după ce a fugit din ţara sa şi a intrat în România, a fost recuperat de către salvamontiştii maramureşeni joi după-amiază, în urma unei intervenţii dificile. „Avea provizii şi haine de schimb, era excelent echipat şi foarte bine pregătit fizic. (...) Ne-a mărturisit că a luptat pe front doi ani şi opt luni, lucru care i-a uşurat tentativa de fugă”, afirmă şeful Salvamont Marammureş. Potrivit acestuia, ucraineanul de 41 de ani a reuşit să suprevieţuiască în munţi, în condiţii extreme, timp de trei nopţi, în prezent fiind în stare bună.

romania.title: Weekend special dedicat copiilor, pe Calea Victoriei, la „Străzi Deschise – București”

Acest demers, în mare parte simbolic, vine pe fondul dezbaterilor din Franța despre despăgubirile pentru sclavie și despre persistența rasismului și a inegalităților …

medical.title: Unexpected allies: Eosinophils may help predict cancer immunotherapy response and survival
medical.summary: Long regarded as cells involved primarily in allergic responses and antiparasitic defense, eosinophils are now drawing increasing attention in oncology. A review article led by Marie Gilon, an oncology resident physician and Ph.D. candidate at the University of Liège, synthesizes current knowledge on how these white blood cells interact with tumor biology and may inform the clinical management of cancer patients.

medical.title: Sleep disorders linked to socioeconomic disparities in Appalachia
medical.summary: Appalachia has a legacy of making the most with limited resources, but there's one thing that there's no way to stretch: sleep.

medical.title: Brain maps reveal first lifetime white matter growth charts from birth to 100
medical.summary: In a new study published recently in the journal Nature, researchers at Vanderbilt University and Vanderbilt Health have created the first growth charts for white matter in the brain over a human lifetime. The work brings together nearly two decades of Vanderbilt research collaborations, the university's extensive MRI data collections, and an advanced AI-enabled computing platform.

medical.title: The Enhanced Games set out to 'transform sport' but the results looked surprisingly ordinary
medical.summary: The Enhanced Games promised a revolution. Athletes on supervised drug regimens, unshackled from the anti-doping rules of the Olympics, were going to show us what the human body was truly capable of. The event was transhumanism in practice—a glimpse at humanity's athletic future.

medical.title: Do lying children grow up to be criminals? Mostly not, but persistent patterns may signal later risk
medical.summary: Most childhood lying does not lead to serious problems in adulthood, and only certain kinds of lying behavior are associated with later psychological or legal issues, a new study has found.

medical.title: Obesity may influence how breast cancer spreads
medical.summary: Obesity may change how early-stage breast cancer becomes invasive, according to a study by University of Oklahoma researchers published in The American Journal of Pathology.

medical.title: Lab-grown heart patch boosts pumping power in severe heart failure trial
medical.summary: Researchers at the University Medical Center Göttingen (UMG) and the University Hospital of Schleswig-Holstein (UKSH) have made a significant breakthrough in the treatment of severe heart failure: For the first time, a clinical trial has demonstrated that laboratory-grown heart muscle tissue can improve the pumping function of damaged hearts.

medical.title: World Cup 2026: Why moving games to evenings isn't enough to tackle extreme heat problem
medical.summary: The 2026 FIFA World Cup is the biggest ever edition of the world's most watched sporting tournament. The 48 teams taking part in Canada, the US and Mexico may find their toughest opponent is the extreme heat.

medical.title: Gene activity clocks estimate lifespan across species, matching epigenetic tools
medical.summary: Molecular clocks that can provide accurate estimates of both molecular age and lifespan across multiple mammalian species and tissue types are presented in an article published in Nature this week. An analysis of more than 11,000 human, rodent, and primate samples reveals conserved signatures of aging. This framework may aid the development of targeted interventions to improve longevity.

medical.title: More support needed for aged care staff to steer end-of-life conversations
medical.summary: A new study from Flinders University researchers describes the perspectives of aged care staff on when and how they communicate with families to plan end-of-life care—and identifies that increased training and resources are needed to properly equip aged care staff for appropriate engagement with families. The work is published in the Australasian Journal on Ageing.

medical.title: At a live concert, your brain locks onto the music more tightly—especially when the tempo is fast
medical.summary: We have high fidelity vinyl and home audio systems that rival the quality of the sound systems at the local movie theater and we even have high-quality recordings that we can listen to on our preferred personal music device. But there's just something about hearing live music as it's being played in real time.

medical.title: Weight-shaming from loved ones during pregnancy can hinder healthy habits
medical.summary: Criticism about their weight from their loved ones—partners, family, and friends—may make it harder for women to stay active and eat well during pregnancy and after giving birth, a University of Alberta study shows. Surveys of 463 women showed that 85% of them felt the sting of weight stigma during pregnancy from the people closest to them, and 74% experienced it after giving birth, with the result that they were less likely to meet nutrition and physical activity guidelines.

medical.title: Seven AI models vote out medical hallucinations in 10,000 chatbot tests
medical.summary: As chatbots powered by artificial intelligence become more ingrained in our everyday lives, people are increasingly using them to help diagnose their medical concerns. Should I be worried about this rash? What if this insect bite gets infected? Is this pain the symptom of a larger problem? When dealing with someone's health, the answers need to be as accurate as possible.

medical.title: Study reveals how people use AI for mental health support—and their concerns about it
medical.summary: As more people turn to artificial intelligence chatbots for emotional and mental health support, a new study from Drexel University posted to the arXiv preprint server suggests that most users see these tools as supplements to—not substitutes for—human therapy. Drawing on millions of Reddit posts, the study highlights both the growing appeal of AI support tools for emotional reassurance, coping and practical guidance, as well as the concerns users express about emotional dependence, misinformation and overreliance on the technology.

medical.title: Routine heart test can track how kids grow and mature, new study finds
medical.summary: A new study from Wake Forest University School of Medicine suggests a routine heart test—an electrocardiogram (ECG)—may offer researchers a new way to measure biological development in children and adolescents.

medical.title: Psychiatric 'gold standard' falters as repeat interviews yield different diagnoses
medical.summary: Diagnostic interviews are widely used by mental health professionals to identify conditions such as anxiety, bipolar disorder and depression in adults, but new research led by McMaster University shows that the long considered "gold standard" may not be as consistent as previously thought.

medical.title: Healthy lifestyle shown to lower risk of death after cancer diagnosis
medical.summary: New evidence shows that sticking to five lifestyle recommendations improves survival after a later cancer diagnosis. The findings provide encouraging evidence that simple, achievable habits established before a cancer diagnosis can play a role in improving long-term health and the health of those following a cancer diagnosis.

medical.title: Depression may not only be a consequence, but also a cause of rheumatoid arthritis
medical.summary: According to researchers at Semmelweis University, not only inflammation, but also sleep disorders, depression, obesity, and smoking may sustain persistent rheumatic symptoms. In their publications in the journals Nature Reviews Rheumatology and The Lancet Rheumatology, they also proposed a model that can help identify and treat the true causes of symptoms in time.

medical.title: Wegovy linked to rare “eye stroke” that can cause sudden blindness
medical.summary: A new analysis is raising concerns about Wegovy, the blockbuster weight-loss drug, after researchers found it may carry the highest risk of a rare “eye stroke” that can cause sudden vision loss. The study, based on millions of FDA side-effect reports, found the risk signal was nearly five times stronger for Wegovy than for Ozempic, despite both containing semaglutide.

science.title: Taking dark energy out of the equation: Mathematicians challenge the standard cosmological model of the universe
science.summary: Mathematicians are challenging the idea that dark energy is responsible for the accelerating expansion of the universe. In a new paper published in Proceedings of the Royal Society A, mathematicians from the University of California, Davis, provide mathematical proof that instabilities inherent in the Einstein-Euler equations imply that the current model of the expanding universe is not viable.

science.title: Mitochondria reveal built-in speed control for protein production
science.summary: Researchers at the University Medical Center Göttingen (UMG) and the Max Planck Institute (MPI) for Multidisciplinary Sciences have elucidated how the production of certain proteins and their insertion into the inner membrane of mitochondria are coordinated.

science.title: Framework could deliver fairer insurance deals for customers
science.summary: Insurance companies could use a new research-based tool to reduce "proxy discrimination" in the pricing models that shape premiums in the sector.

science.title: Contractor to Civil Servant: NASA Welcomes Kenny Heckle
science.summary: Kenny Heckle grew up in Orlando, just west of NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. An 80s child, he comes from a long line of union pipefitters and fabricators. Heckle recalls the day 42 years ago at KARS Park, which is a NASA Exchange–run recreation area for the agency’s workforce and their guests, when he […]

science.title: NASA Astronaut Andrew Morgan Retires
science.summary: May 28, 2026 After a 12-year career at NASA, U.S. Army Brig. Gen. Andrew R. Morgan has retired from the agency to continue his military service. Morgan spent 272 days in space aboard the International Space Station. NASA selected Morgan to join its 21st astronaut class in August 2013. He launched to the space station […]

science.title: One in six kids could be experiencing online sexual exploitation and abuse
science.summary: One in six internet-using children from a survey of nearly 12,000 children in 12 countries across Asia and Africa are found to experience at least one form of technology-facilitated sexual exploitation and abuse, with many experiences undisclosed, according to research published in Nature. The findings may help to inform prevention and response efforts where coordinated action by policymakers, law enforcement, and technology companies is urgently needed to protect children.

science.title: From the seabed to Mars: Why geological maps matter
science.summary: From Australia's remote deserts to the surface of Mars, geological mapping underpins how we understand landscapes, natural resources, and the processes that shape our planet and others beyond it.

science.title: Quantum pendulum clock overcomes classical accuracy limits and sheds light on quantum to classical transitions
science.summary: In a grandfather clock, a pendulum swings back and forth and this periodic motion is maintained using the energy stored in its suspended weights. This is done with the help of the escapement mechanism, which converts the gravitational energy of the weights into impulses that drive the pendulum, which then moves the clock's gears, which move its hands.

science.title: An AI solution to an 80‑year‑old problem has shocked mathematicians
science.summary: Last week, OpenAI shocked the mathematical community by revealing that one of its internal artificial intelligence (AI) models had found a counterexample to a famous conjecture made by legendary Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős in 1946.

science.title: Some experts now say psychopathy doesn't exist—here's why we may be looking at it all wrong
science.summary: As an expert on personality disorders, people often ask me about psychopathy. It seems everybody has had an ex, a boss, a neighbor or a relative who they suspect has traits of it. People are curious about how to recognize psychopathy, and whether it can explain certain harmful behavior. It's easy to see why. Psychopathic people are everywhere—from books and movies to newspaper articles and academic papers.

science.title: Introduced wild pigs linked to fewer invasive plants, while native deer show the opposite pattern
science.summary: Wild pigs are generally considered among the world's most problematic invasive mammals. But a major new study from Aarhus Universitet shows that the introduced animals may actually have beneficial effects in North American forests.

science.title: Study finds soils can naturally suppress major crop diseases
science.summary: Curtin University researchers have discovered some agricultural soils can naturally suppress one of Australia's most damaging broadacre crop diseases. The research, published in the journal Applied Soil Ecology, found some soils can inhibit infection and survival of Sclerotinia sclerotiorum—the fungus responsible for Sclerotinia stem rot, a disease that causes significant yield losses in broadacre crops such as canola and pulses.

science.title: Cobalt honeycombs open a new path to quantum computing
science.summary: Honeycombs are famous for their elegant design, but now they may have found a new application: quantum computing. To collect knowledge from subatomic particles, quantum computers require carefully designed materials capable of performing necessary, complex functions. However, the metals used, such as ruthenium and iridium, are often rare and expensive, limiting the potential to build new technology.

science.title: Teen sex survey highlights violence concerns
science.summary: Australian teens are experiencing high rates of intimate partner violence, one in 10 are relying on the withdrawal method for contraception and many don't know how to access help for their sexual health, according to the country's largest study of adolescents' sexual health and relationships.

science.title: Think DEET keeps mosquitoes away? They may be learning to love it
science.summary: Every summer, millions of people spray themselves with DEET to keep mosquitoes away. But new research suggests mosquitoes may be able to learn to associate the repellent with food—and even become attracted to it.

science.title: Findings could justify greater protections for minks in Everglades
science.summary: Their fur might feel the same, but a mink in the Everglades is not the same as a mink from northern Florida, researchers from the University of Connecticut and Central Connecticut State University report in the April 20 issue of Nature Heredity, which also ran a podcast discussing the research. The scientists' findings could justify greater protections for the Everglades mink.

science.title: NASA’s Roman Mission Preps to Unveil New Populations of Faraway Worlds
science.summary: NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope is poised to make a major leap in the hunt for worlds outside our solar system, known as exoplanets. Scientists expect the mission to reveal around 100,000 worlds — a staggering leap compared to the nearly 6,300 found so far thanks to NASA missions working in tandem with other […]

science.title: Platform fast-tracks microbial design for high-temp manufacturing
science.summary: Scientists at the Department of Energy's Oak Ridge National Laboratory have developed a platform that engineers heat-loving microbes for industrial-scale manufacturing in a matter of weeks compared with previous approaches that can take months to years. This biotechnology tool strengthens American manufacturing capacity, supporting long-term energy security and global competitiveness.

science.title: Green stones buried with Panama's ancient chiefs confirmed as Colombian emeralds
science.summary: More than 1,000 years ago, Panama elites were buried together with translucent green stones long suspected to be emeralds. However, scientific analysis confirming the suspicion has never been conducted. Now, scientists have confirmed for the first time that these green gems were emeralds that traveled more than 700 km through complex trade networks linking Central and South American societies.

science.title: How NASA Uses Light to Detect Waste From Mines
science.summary: Tens of thousands of abandoned mines threaten waterways across the American West, but identifying which sites urgently need cleanup is slow and expensive. Now, NASA’s EMIT instrument can analyze the unique light signatures of mine waste from space to help focus remediation efforts where they’re needed most.

science.title: Scientists discover the perfect temperature to keep mangoes fresh much longer
science.summary: Scientists found that storing mangoes at 54°F dramatically slows ripening and keeps the fruit fresh far longer than typical tropical temperatures. The cooler conditions helped mangoes stay firm, retain moisture, and preserve important antioxidants while reducing cellular damage. Researchers also uncovered the internal defense systems that switch on during cold storage, protecting the fruit from stress and decay.

science.title: NASA stunned as strange solar radio burst lasts 19 days
science.summary: NASA scientists were stunned when a strange radio signal from the Sun refused to fade away. Instead of lasting a few hours or days like normal solar radio bursts, this one persisted for an astonishing 19 days — shattering the previous record. Using a fleet of spacecraft spread across the solar system, researchers tracked the mysterious signal to a massive magnetic structure on the Sun called a helmet streamer.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: CM 2026. SUA, Mexic şi Canada anunţă măsuri de sănătate publică privind călătoriile, în contextul epidemiei de Ebola
mix_items.summary: Statele Unite, Mexic şi Canada au anunţat joi măsuri coordonate de sănătate publică privind călătoriile pentru persoanele care sosesc din regiunile africane expuse celui mai mare risc de infectare cu Ebola. Autorităţile din cele trei ţări au emis o declaraţie comună, în scopul de a proteja cetăţenii şi vizitatorii pe durata Cupei Mondiale de fotbal, relatează Reuters.

mix_items.title: Portugalia înregistrează un record de temperatură pentru o zi de mai, de peste 40 de grade Celsius
mix_items.summary: Portugalia a înregistrat un nou record de căldură pentru o zi din luna mai, cu o temperatură maximală de 40,3 grade Celsius măsurată miercuri în Mora, un oraş din centrul ţării, a anunţat joi agenţia meteorologică portugheză, precizând că precedentul record, care era de 40 de grade Celsius, fusese stabilit în mai 2001, informează agențiile …

mix_items.title: Weekend special dedicat copiilor, pe Calea Victoriei, la „Străzi Deschise – București”
mix_items.summary: În weekendul 30–31 mai, „Străzi Deschise – București, Promenadă urbană” transformă Calea Victoriei într-un univers al jocului, creativității și bucuriei, printr-o ediție specială dedicată Zilei Internaționale a Copilului. Timp de… Articolul Weekend special dedicat copiilor, pe Calea Victoriei, la „Străzi Deschise – București” apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Ucrainean rătăcit în Munţii Maramureşului, recuperat: „Era excelent echipat şi foarte bine pregătit fizic, a luptat pe front”
mix_items.summary: Ucraineanul rătăcit într-o zonă de prăpăstii şi cascade din Munţii Maramureşului, după ce a fugit din ţara sa şi a intrat în România, a fost recuperat de către salvamontiştii maramureşeni joi după-amiază, în urma unei intervenţii dificile. „Avea provizii şi haine de schimb, era excelent echipat şi foarte bine pregătit fizic. (...) Ne-a mărturisit că a luptat pe front doi ani şi opt luni, lucru care i-a uşurat tentativa de fugă”, afirmă şeful Salvamont Marammureş. Potrivit acestuia, ucraineanul de 41 de ani a reuşit să suprevieţuiască în munţi, în condiţii extreme, timp de trei nopţi, în prezent fiind în stare bună.

mix_items.title: Youni4youth, conferinta educationala dedicata antreprenoriatului si gestionarea unei afaceri, va avea loc pe 7 lunie la Nord Events Center by Globalworth
mix_items.summary: Youni4 Youth, conferintà educationala dedicata elevilor si studentilor interesati de modul în care se gestioneaza o afacere si ce consta in a fi un antreprenor de succes. mix_items.summary: De azi înainte, răspunsul la întrebarea „Câte cetăţenii are Traian Băsescu” e clasicul „Trei, în p… mea”. Pe lângă paşaportul românesc şi cel moldovenesc, fostul preşedinte al României a primit recent şi cetăţenie scoţiană. Cel mai probabil, autorităţile scoţiene i-au oferit lui Băsescu cetăţenia în semn de recunoştinţă, după ce acesta a salvat economia ţării […] The post Scoţia îi oferă şi ea cetăţenie lui Băsescu: „A băgat milioane în economia noastră!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Deputații francezi au aprobat abrogarea legilor rușinoase din perioada regelui Ludovic al XIV-lea
mix_items.summary: Deputații francezi au votat joi pentru abrogarea oficială a unor legi din epoca sclaviei care defineau statutul juridic al persoanelor înrobite drept „bunuri mobile” și justificau abuzurile și pedepsele corporale, relatează Reuters. Acest demers, în mare parte simbolic, vine pe fondul dezbaterilor din Franța despre despăgubirile pentru sclavie și despre persistența rasismului și a inegalităților …

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Unexpected allies: Eosinophils may help predict cancer immunotherapy response and survival
mix_items.summary: Long regarded as cells involved primarily in allergic responses and antiparasitic defense, eosinophils are now drawing increasing attention in oncology. A review article led by Marie Gilon, an oncology resident physician and Ph.D. candidate at the University of Liège, synthesizes current knowledge on how these white blood cells interact with tumor biology and may inform the clinical management of cancer patients.

mix_items.summary: Appalachia has a legacy of making the most with limited resources, but there's one thing that there's no way to stretch: sleep.

mix_items.title: Taking dark energy out of the equation: Mathematicians challenge the standard cosmological model of the universe
mix_items.summary: Mathematicians are challenging the idea that dark energy is responsible for the accelerating expansion of the universe. In a new paper published in Proceedings of the Royal Society A, mathematicians from the University of California, Davis, provide mathematical proof that instabilities inherent in the Einstein-Euler equations imply that the current model of the expanding universe is not viable.

mix_items.title: Brain maps reveal first lifetime white matter growth charts from birth to 100
mix_items.summary: In a new study published recently in the journal Nature, researchers at Vanderbilt University and Vanderbilt Health have created the first growth charts for white matter in the brain over a human lifetime. The work brings together nearly two decades of Vanderbilt research collaborations, the university's extensive MRI data collections, and an advanced AI-enabled computing platform.

mix_items.summary: The Enhanced Games promised a revolution. Athletes on supervised drug regimens, unshackled from the anti-doping rules of the Olympics, were going to show us what the human body was truly capable of. The event was transhumanism in practice—a glimpse at humanity's athletic future.

mix_items.title: Mitochondria reveal built-in speed control for protein production
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University Medical Center Göttingen (UMG) and the Max Planck Institute (MPI) for Multidisciplinary Sciences have elucidated how the production of certain proteins and their insertion into the inner membrane of mitochondria are coordinated.

mix_items.title: Do lying children grow up to be criminals? Mostly not, but persistent patterns may signal later risk
mix_items.summary: Most childhood lying does not lead to serious problems in adulthood, and only certain kinds of lying behavior are associated with later psychological or legal issues, a new study has found.

mix_items.title: Obesity may influence how breast cancer spreads
mix_items.summary: Obesity may change how early-stage breast cancer becomes invasive, according to a study by University of Oklahoma researchers published in The American Journal of Pathology.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Framework could deliver fairer insurance deals for customers
mix_items.summary: Insurance companies could use a new research-based tool to reduce "proxy discrimination" in the pricing models that shape premiums in the sector.

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Contractor to Civil Servant: NASA Welcomes Kenny Heckle
mix_items.summary: Kenny Heckle grew up in Orlando, just west of NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. An 80s child, he comes from a long line of union pipefitters and fabricators. Heckle recalls the day 42 years ago at KARS Park, which is a NASA Exchange–run recreation area for the agency’s workforce and their guests, when he […]

mix_items.title: NASA Astronaut Andrew Morgan Retires
mix_items.summary: May 28, 2026 After a 12-year career at NASA, U.S. Army Brig. Gen. Andrew R. Morgan has retired from the agency to continue his military service. Morgan spent 272 days in space aboard the International Space Station. NASA selected Morgan to join its 21st astronaut class in August 2013. He launched to the space station […]

mix_items.title: Lab-grown heart patch boosts pumping power in severe heart failure trial
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University Medical Center Göttingen (UMG) and the University Hospital of Schleswig-Holstein (UKSH) have made a significant breakthrough in the treatment of severe heart failure: For the first time, a clinical trial has demonstrated that laboratory-grown heart muscle tissue can improve the pumping function of damaged hearts.

mix_items.title: One in six kids could be experiencing online sexual exploitation and abuse
mix_items.summary: One in six internet-using children from a survey of nearly 12,000 children in 12 countries across Asia and Africa are found to experience at least one form of technology-facilitated sexual exploitation and abuse, with many experiences undisclosed, according to research published in Nature. The findings may help to inform prevention and response efforts where coordinated action by policymakers, law enforcement, and technology companies is urgently needed to protect children.

mix_items.title: From the seabed to Mars: Why geological maps matter
mix_items.summary: From Australia's remote deserts to the surface of Mars, geological mapping underpins how we understand landscapes, natural resources, and the processes that shape our planet and others beyond it.

mix_items.title: Quantum pendulum clock overcomes classical accuracy limits and sheds light on quantum to classical transitions
mix_items.summary: In a grandfather clock, a pendulum swings back and forth and this periodic motion is maintained using the energy stored in its suspended weights. This is done with the help of the escapement mechanism, which converts the gravitational energy of the weights into impulses that drive the pendulum, which then moves the clock's gears, which move its hands.

mix_items.title: World Cup 2026: Why moving games to evenings isn't enough to tackle extreme heat problem
mix_items.summary: The 2026 FIFA World Cup is the biggest ever edition of the world's most watched sporting tournament. The 48 teams taking part in Canada, the US and Mexico may find their toughest opponent is the extreme heat.

mix_items.title: An AI solution to an 80‑year‑old problem has shocked mathematicians
mix_items.summary: Last week, OpenAI shocked the mathematical community by revealing that one of its internal artificial intelligence (AI) models had found a counterexample to a famous conjecture made by legendary Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős in 1946.

mix_items.title: Gene activity clocks estimate lifespan across species, matching epigenetic tools
mix_items.summary: Molecular clocks that can provide accurate estimates of both molecular age and lifespan across multiple mammalian species and tissue types are presented in an article published in Nature this week. An analysis of more than 11,000 human, rodent, and primate samples reveals conserved signatures of aging. This framework may aid the development of targeted interventions to improve longevity.

mix_items.title: More support needed for aged care staff to steer end-of-life conversations
mix_items.summary: A new study from Flinders University researchers describes the perspectives of aged care staff on when and how they communicate with families to plan end-of-life care—and identifies that increased training and resources are needed to properly equip aged care staff for appropriate engagement with families. The work is published in the Australasian Journal on Ageing.

mix_items.title: Some experts now say psychopathy doesn't exist—here's why we may be looking at it all wrong
mix_items.summary: As an expert on personality disorders, people often ask me about psychopathy. It seems everybody has had an ex, a boss, a neighbor or a relative who they suspect has traits of it. People are curious about how to recognize psychopathy, and whether it can explain certain harmful behavior. It's easy to see why. Psychopathic people are everywhere—from books and movies to newspaper articles and academic papers.

mix_items.title: At a live concert, your brain locks onto the music more tightly—especially when the tempo is fast
mix_items.summary: We have high fidelity vinyl and home audio systems that rival the quality of the sound systems at the local movie theater and we even have high-quality recordings that we can listen to on our preferred personal music device. But there's just something about hearing live music as it's being played in real time.

mix_items.title: Weight-shaming from loved ones during pregnancy can hinder healthy habits
mix_items.summary: Criticism about their weight from their loved ones—partners, family, and friends—may make it harder for women to stay active and eat well during pregnancy and after giving birth, a University of Alberta study shows. Surveys of 463 women showed that 85% of them felt the sting of weight stigma during pregnancy from the people closest to them, and 74% experienced it after giving birth, with the result that they were less likely to meet nutrition and physical activity guidelines.

mix_items.title: Seven AI models vote out medical hallucinations in 10,000 chatbot tests
mix_items.summary: As chatbots powered by artificial intelligence become more ingrained in our everyday lives, people are increasingly using them to help diagnose their medical concerns. Should I be worried about this rash? What if this insect bite gets infected? Is this pain the symptom of a larger problem? When dealing with someone's health, the answers need to be as accurate as possible.

mix_items.title: Introduced wild pigs linked to fewer invasive plants, while native deer show the opposite pattern
mix_items.summary: Wild pigs are generally considered among the world's most problematic invasive mammals. But a major new study from Aarhus Universitet shows that the introduced animals may actually have beneficial effects in North American forests.

mix_items.title: Study finds soils can naturally suppress major crop diseases
mix_items.summary: Curtin University researchers have discovered some agricultural soils can naturally suppress one of Australia's most damaging broadacre crop diseases. The research, published in the journal Applied Soil Ecology, found some soils can inhibit infection and survival of Sclerotinia sclerotiorum—the fungus responsible for Sclerotinia stem rot, a disease that causes significant yield losses in broadacre crops such as canola and pulses.

mix_items.title: Study reveals how people use AI for mental health support—and their concerns about it
mix_items.summary: As more people turn to artificial intelligence chatbots for emotional and mental health support, a new study from Drexel University posted to the arXiv preprint server suggests that most users see these tools as supplements to—not substitutes for—human therapy. Drawing on millions of Reddit posts, the study highlights both the growing appeal of AI support tools for emotional reassurance, coping and practical guidance, as well as the concerns users express about emotional dependence, misinformation and overreliance on the technology.

mix_items.title: Cobalt honeycombs open a new path to quantum computing
mix_items.summary: Honeycombs are famous for their elegant design, but now they may have found a new application: quantum computing. To collect knowledge from subatomic particles, quantum computers require carefully designed materials capable of performing necessary, complex functions. However, the metals used, such as ruthenium and iridium, are often rare and expensive, limiting the potential to build new technology.

mix_items.title: Routine heart test can track how kids grow and mature, new study finds
mix_items.summary: A new study from Wake Forest University School of Medicine suggests a routine heart test—an electrocardiogram (ECG)—may offer researchers a new way to measure biological development in children and adolescents.

mix_items.title: Teen sex survey highlights violence concerns
mix_items.summary: Australian teens are experiencing high rates of intimate partner violence, one in 10 are relying on the withdrawal method for contraception and many don't know how to access help for their sexual health, according to the country's largest study of adolescents' sexual health and relationships.

mix_items.title: Think DEET keeps mosquitoes away? They may be learning to love it
mix_items.summary: Every summer, millions of people spray themselves with DEET to keep mosquitoes away. But new research suggests mosquitoes may be able to learn to associate the repellent with food—and even become attracted to it.

mix_items.title: Findings could justify greater protections for minks in Everglades
mix_items.summary: Their fur might feel the same, but a mink in the Everglades is not the same as a mink from northern Florida, researchers from the University of Connecticut and Central Connecticut State University report in the April 20 issue of Nature Heredity, which also ran a podcast discussing the research. The scientists' findings could justify greater protections for the Everglades mink.

mix_items.title: Psychiatric 'gold standard' falters as repeat interviews yield different diagnoses
mix_items.summary: Diagnostic interviews are widely used by mental health professionals to identify conditions such as anxiety, bipolar disorder and depression in adults, but new research led by McMaster University shows that the long considered "gold standard" may not be as consistent as previously thought.

mix_items.title: Healthy lifestyle shown to lower risk of death after cancer diagnosis
mix_items.summary: New evidence shows that sticking to five lifestyle recommendations improves survival after a later cancer diagnosis. The findings provide encouraging evidence that simple, achievable habits established before a cancer diagnosis can play a role in improving long-term health and the health of those following a cancer diagnosis.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Roman Mission Preps to Unveil New Populations of Faraway Worlds
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope is poised to make a major leap in the hunt for worlds outside our solar system, known as exoplanets. Scientists expect the mission to reveal around 100,000 worlds — a staggering leap compared to the nearly 6,300 found so far thanks to NASA missions working in tandem with other […]

mix_items.title: Depression may not only be a consequence, but also a cause of rheumatoid arthritis
mix_items.summary: According to researchers at Semmelweis University, not only inflammation, but also sleep disorders, depression, obesity, and smoking may sustain persistent rheumatic symptoms. In their publications in the journals Nature Reviews Rheumatology and The Lancet Rheumatology, they also proposed a model that can help identify and treat the true causes of symptoms in time.

mix_items.title: Platform fast-tracks microbial design for high-temp manufacturing
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the Department of Energy's Oak Ridge National Laboratory have developed a platform that engineers heat-loving microbes for industrial-scale manufacturing in a matter of weeks compared with previous approaches that can take months to years. This biotechnology tool strengthens American manufacturing capacity, supporting long-term energy security and global competitiveness.

mix_items.title: Green stones buried with Panama's ancient chiefs confirmed as Colombian emeralds
mix_items.summary: More than 1,000 years ago, Panama elites were buried together with translucent green stones long suspected to be emeralds. However, scientific analysis confirming the suspicion has never been conducted. Now, scientists have confirmed for the first time that these green gems were emeralds that traveled more than 700 km through complex trade networks linking Central and South American societies.

mix_items.title: How NASA Uses Light to Detect Waste From Mines
mix_items.summary: Tens of thousands of abandoned mines threaten waterways across the American West, but identifying which sites urgently need cleanup is slow and expensive. Now, NASA’s EMIT instrument can analyze the unique light signatures of mine waste from space to help focus remediation efforts where they’re needed most.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the perfect temperature to keep mangoes fresh much longer
mix_items.summary: Scientists found that storing mangoes at 54°F dramatically slows ripening and keeps the fruit fresh far longer than typical tropical temperatures. The cooler conditions helped mangoes stay firm, retain moisture, and preserve important antioxidants while reducing cellular damage. Researchers also uncovered the internal defense systems that switch on during cold storage, protecting the fruit from stress and decay.

mix_items.title: Wegovy linked to rare “eye stroke” that can cause sudden blindness
mix_items.summary: A new analysis is raising concerns about Wegovy, the blockbuster weight-loss drug, after researchers found it may carry the highest risk of a rare “eye stroke” that can cause sudden vision loss. The study, based on millions of FDA side-effect reports, found the risk signal was nearly five times stronger for Wegovy than for Ozempic, despite both containing semaglutide.

mix_items.title: NASA stunned as strange solar radio burst lasts 19 days
mix_items.summary: NASA scientists were stunned when a strange radio signal from the Sun refused to fade away. Instead of lasting a few hours or days like normal solar radio bursts, this one persisted for an astonishing 19 days — shattering the previous record. Using a fleet of spacecraft spread across the solar system, researchers tracked the mysterious signal to a massive magnetic structure on the Sun called a helmet streamer.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Telefonul are 3% baterie, dar incredere maxima in mine.

satire_ro.title: Scoţia îi oferă şi ea cetăţenie lui Băsescu: „A băgat milioane în economia noastră!”
